name: Calvert Cliffs State Park
short_description: Maryland's iconic Miocene fossil treasure hunting site. Hunt for shark teeth, fossil shells, and marine specimens along dramatic cliff faces on the Patuxent River.
description:
Calvert Cliffs State Park is Maryland's premier fossil hunting destination, famous for its 40-foot tall cliffs composed of Miocene-age deposits packed with marine fossils dating back 15-20 million years. The park sits along the Patuxent River and offers public access to a 1.4-mile hiking trail that descends to the shoreline and cliff base where the best fossil collecting occurs. The exposed geology here is part of the Calvert Cliffs Formation, a world-renowned geological unit that has yielded thousands of specimens including shark teeth (particularly from the extinct Carcharodon megalodon), dolphin vertebrae, whale bones, and intricate shell fossils. Visitors can freely collect fossils found loose along the beach and cliff debris, though collection directly from the cliff face itself is prohibited to prevent erosion and safety hazards.

The trail is relatively easy and well-maintained, descending gradually through a wooded forest before opening to the river beach. The cliffs themselves rise dramatically from the water's edge, creating a spectacular scenic backdrop. The best fossil hunting occurs along the base of the cliffs where natural weathering constantly exposes new material. The sandy and silty beach is easy to walk on, though the return hike gains elevation steadily. Spring and fall offer the most comfortable collecting conditions, though the site is accessible year-round.

This is an exceptional location for both beginners and experienced collectors. The sheer abundance of fossils means newcomers can find specimens with minimal effort, while serious paleontology enthusiasts often discover rare or scientifically valuable pieces. The combination of easy access, proven productivity, scenic beauty, and educational value makes Calvert Cliffs a destination rockhound site on the East Coast.
history: The Calvert Cliffs Formation was deposited during the Miocene epoch (approximately 15-20 million years ago) when this region was submerged beneath a warm, shallow marine sea. Fossil-bearing sediments accumulated from a rich ecosystem of sharks, rays, whales, dolphins, and countless mollusks. These deposits have made Calvert Cliffs one of the most important paleontological sites on the East Coast and a major reference point for understanding Atlantic Coast Miocene paleontology.
tips: Focus your search along the base of the cliffs where fresh material is constantly exposed by erosion and tidal action; never dig into the cliff face itself as this is prohibited and dangerous. Look for dark brown to black shark teeth often embedded in the tan and gray sandy sediments, paying special attention to small curved objects and glossy surfaces. Bring a small brush and container to gently extract and store specimens; many fossils are delicate and require careful handling. Visit after storms or heavy rains when cliff runoff exposes new material, and time your visit for low tide to access the maximum beach area.
terrain_notes: The park sits at virtually sea level (3 feet elevation) with flat terrain. The main fossil trail is 1.4 miles round trip, well-groomed and gradually descending through deciduous forest before reaching the riverside beach. The final descent to the cliff base involves some scrambling over loose sediment and rock debris. The beach itself is sandy and relatively flat, though wet clay and silt can be slippery. Return hike involves steady uphill walking on the trail. Total walking distance from parking lot to cliff base is approximately 0.7 miles.
best_season: March - May and September - November offer ideal conditions with moderate temperatures and lower humidity. Summer months (June - August) are hot and humid with higher visitation. Winter is accessible but can be cold and muddy. Low tide windows are crucial for beach access, so check tide tables before visiting.
written_directions: From Lusby, take Maryland Route 2/4 south toward Solomons. Turn east on Cliff Road and follow signs for Calvert Cliffs State Park. The main entrance and parking area is approximately 2 miles from Route 2/4. The park has a large gravel parking lot adjacent to the visitor center and trailhead.
road_conditions: Paved road (Cliff Road) to park entrance. Parking area is gravel with good drainage.
parking_notes: Large gravel parking lot with capacity for 50+ vehicles. Located adjacent to visitor center and park facilities. Restroom and picnic facilities available.
collection_rules: Fossils found loose on the beach and ground surface may be collected for personal use only. Collection directly from the cliff face is strictly prohibited due to erosion and safety hazards. No excavation or hammering into the cliffs. No commercial collecting. Maximum reasonable personal collection is typically interpreted as what one person can reasonably carry.
rules:
Fossils loose on beach and ground surface only
No collection from cliff face
No excavation or hammering
Personal use only
No commercial collection
Stay on designated trails
Do not disturb cliff stability
nearest_services: Lusby has gas stations and restaurants approximately 2 miles away. Solomons (4 miles south) offers more dining and supply options. No water available at the park itself.
accessibility_notes: The main trail is relatively flat and well-maintained but not wheelchair accessible due to the final descent to the beach. The 1.4-mile round trip trail has modest elevation changes (total descent approximately 80 feet). Sturdy footwear is essential as the beach includes sandy areas, silt, and loose rock debris. The trail is shaded for most of the hike, providing relief in summer months. Younger children may need assistance on steeper sections near the cliff base.

faq.question: What fossils can I find at Calvert Cliffs State Park in Maryland?
faq.answer: Calvert Cliffs is famous for Miocene-age fossils dating back 15-20 million years. The most common finds are shark teeth (especially the extinct megalodon), dolphin vertebrae, whale bones, and shells. Many visitors find 5-20 specimens per visit along the cliff base and beach.

faq.question: Is there a fee to collect fossils at Calvert Cliffs State Park?
faq.answer: No entry fee. Calvert Cliffs State Park offers free public access. You can collect fossils found loose on the beach and ground surface at no cost. The only restriction is that you cannot collect from the cliff face itself.

faq.question: Do I need a permit to rockhound at Calvert Cliffs in Calvert County, Maryland?
faq.answer: No permit is required for personal fossil collection at Calvert Cliffs State Park. Simply follow the collection rules: collect only loose material from the beach, avoid the cliff face, and limit your collection to what you can reasonably carry.

faq.question: What is the best time of year to find fossils at Calvert Cliffs?
faq.answer: March through May and September through November offer ideal conditions with moderate temperatures and comfortable hiking weather. Visit during low tide for maximum beach access. Fresh material is exposed after heavy rains and storms year-round.

faq.question: How difficult is the hike to fossil hunting areas at Calvert Cliffs State Park?
faq.answer: The trail is easy and beginner-friendly. The main 1.4-mile round trip path is well-maintained and gradually descends through forest to the beach. While there is a modest elevation change of about 80 feet, families with children and most fitness levels can complete it comfortably.

faq.question: Are dogs allowed at Calvert Cliffs State Park rockhounding sites?
faq.answer: Yes, leashed dogs are permitted on the trails and beach at Calvert Cliffs State Park. Keep dogs under control near the cliff base due to unstable debris and the proximity to the water's edge.

meta_title: Calvert Cliffs State Park Fossil Hunting - Maryland
meta_description: Free fossil hunting at Maryland's iconic Calvert Cliffs. Find shark teeth, shells, and marine fossils. Easy trail, scenic cliffs, no permit required.
